title: Quiz: Measurement Error Mitigation
description: Test your understanding of measurement error mitigation

questions.question: What does the confusion matrix M[i,j] represent?

options.text: Probability of preparing state j given measurement i
Probability of measuring state i given prepared state j
Overlap between states i and j

questions.explanation: M[i,j] = P(measure i | prepared j) represents the probability of measuring outcome i when state j was prepared.

questions.question: How do you recover ideal probabilities from noisy measurements?
questions.explanation: If p_noisy = M·p_ideal, then p_ideal = M⁻¹·p_noisy. We invert the confusion matrix.

questions.question: How does the confusion matrix size scale with number of qubits n?
questions.explanation: The full confusion matrix is 2^n × 2^n, one row/column for each computational basis state.

questions.question: Tensored mitigation assumes measurement errors on different qubits are independent.
questions.explanation: True. Tensored mitigation uses M_total = M_1 ⊗ M_2 ⊗ ... which assumes independent errors per qubit.

questions.question: What issue can arise when inverting the confusion matrix?

options.text: May produce negative probabilities requiring clipping

questions.explanation: Matrix inversion can produce negative values that are not valid probabilities, requiring clipping and renormalization.

questions.question: What is needed to calibrate a single-qubit confusion matrix?

options.text: Prepare and measure one random state

questions.explanation: Both basis states |0⟩ and |1⟩ must be prepared and measured to determine P(1|0) and P(0|1).

questions.question: Why might iterative methods be preferred over direct matrix inversion?

options.text: They avoid storing and inverting large matrices

questions.explanation: Iterative methods avoid O(2^n × 2^n) matrix storage and O(8^n) inversion, making them scalable to more qubits.

questions.question: Which are true about measurement error mitigation? (Select all)

options.text: It can be combined with ZNE

questions.explanation: Measurement mitigation is post-processing applied after execution, requires calibration, and can be combined with other techniques. It doesn't correct gate errors.
